title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: ’ll use small DEM Moonshine Peak Idaho, included alongside article. Let’s load raster aggregate factor 25 (chosen make method differences clearly visible). bit cleanup aggregation also helpful. terra::aggregate() default expands coarse raster’s extent input’s dimensions aren’t divisible fact, leaving partial blocks right bottom edges. partial blocks complicate residual analysis later article, ’ll trim truth whole-block dimensions aggregating.
code: truth <- rast("moonshine.tif") |> setNames("truth") fact <- 25L  # Trim to dimensions exactly divisible by `fact` truth[((nrow(truth) %/% fact) * fact + 1):nrow(truth), ] <- NA truth[, ((ncol(truth) %/% fact) * fact + 1):ncol(truth)] <- NA truth <- trim(truth)  coarse <- aggregate(truth, fact, fun = "mean", na.rm = TRUE)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: run five disaggregation methods: two standard ones terra (nearest-neighbor bilinear), three mass-preserving methods terraces.
code: # Standard terra methods nn <- disagg(coarse, fact, method = "near") bl <- disagg(coarse, fact, method = "bilinear")  # terraces mass-preserving methods bl_ac  <- disagg_bl( coarse, fact) cub_ac <- disagg_cub(coarse, fact) pyc_ac <- disagg_pyc(coarse, fact)  # Stack all outputs with the truth raster, aligned to the same extent r <- rast(c(nearest      = nn,             std_bilinear = bl,             ac_bilinear  = bl_ac,             ac_cubic     = cub_ac,             ac_pycno     = pyc_ac)) r <- crop(r, crop(truth, r)) r <- c(crop(truth, r), r)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: Let’s take look disaggregated results. ’ll start maps get high-level sense differences, look transect visualize shapes fitted disaggregation functions.

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: plot chunk 2d-maps first row shows true high-res DEM coarse aggregated raster (visually identical nearest-neighbor disaggregation), maps show estimates generated disaggregation methods. Standard bilinear interpolation via terra::disagg(method = "bilinear") produces image ’s less sharp others: underestimates peak elevation along north-south ridgeline, less contrast ridge-valley systems center image. disagg_bl(), corrects , like standard bilinear show obvious visual artifacts rectangular grid, sharp grid-aligned features along main north-south ridgeline. disagg_cub() disagg_pyc() visually indistinguishable scale. Like disagg_bl(), recover true topographic range standard bilinear interpolation, produce smoother outputs without sharp grid-aligned peaks. course, none methods recovers fine-scale detail true raster, information content genuinely lost aggregation.
code: dr <- as.data.frame(r, xy = TRUE) |>       pivot_longer(-c(x, y), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>       mutate(metric = factor(metric, levels = method_levels,                               labels = method_labels))  ggplot(dr, aes(x, y, fill = value)) +       geom_raster() +       facet_wrap(~ metric, ncol = 2) +       scale_fill_gradientn(colors = c("black", scales::pal_viridis()(12),                                        "orange", "red", "darkred", "black")) +       coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE) +       guides(fill = guide_colorbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = .5)) +       theme(axis.title = element_blank(),             axis.text  = element_blank(),             axis.ticks = element_blank(),             legend.position = "top") +       labs(fill = "Elevation (m) ")

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: 1D transect middle raster makes methods’ fine-scale behavior easier compare. make visualization cleaner, ’ll grab column goes centers coarse cells bilinear fits genuinely 1D, ’ll look just middle section transect. plot chunk transect original ground truth (black) coarse-cell aggregated staircase (gray) show input data. Standard bilinear (red) passes centers staircase steps cleanly, otherwise underrepresents true terrain variability, smoothing peaks filling valleys. Pre-sharpened bilinear (blue) sharp peaks valleys horizontal locations standard bilinear, tracks truth closely since accounts fact coarse values represent block means rather point samples. pycnophylactic (purple) pre-sharpened cubic (green) methods also respect block means, smoother pre-sharpened bilinear peaks valleys snapped coarse cell centers; two curves extremely similar example.
code: # Select column near the middle of raster that aligns with coarse cell centers xs             <- sort(unique(dr$x)) n_coarse_cols  <- length(xs) %/% fact mid_coarse_col <- n_coarse_cols %/% 2L center_idx     <- (mid_coarse_col - 1L) * fact + (fact + 1L) %/% 2L transect <- filter(dr, x == xs[center_idx])  # Zoom in to central chunk of transect transect <- filter(transect, between(y, 42.55, 42.58))  ggplot(transect, aes(y, value, color = metric)) +       geom_ribbon(data = filter(transect, metric == "truth"),                   aes(ymin = 1750, ymax = value),                    fill = "black", alpha = 1, color = NA) +       geom_line() +       scale_color_manual(values = c("black", "gray50", "red", "purple", "dodgerblue", "forestgreen")) +       theme_minimal() +       labs(x = "Latitude (deg)", y = "Elevation (m)", color = NULL)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: pre-sharpening methods (disagg_bl() disagg_cub()) use kernel filters two different scales: pre-sharpening filter operating coarse raster broader kernel, disaggregation kernel operating fine-scale raster relatively narrow kernel. Cells either kernels overlaps raster boundary subject edge effects increase uncertainty results. edge_effects() function maps affected zone, useful need know cells influenced uncertainty. plot chunk edge-effects Values 1 indicate cells within pre-sharpening kernel radius focal pass uses reflective padding; values 2 indicate cells also within interpolator’s reach boundary disaggregation falls back lower-order interpolation. zones slightly increased mass-preservation error compared interior, level-2 band worse two far. implication possible, run algorithms slightly larger region key study area, study area free edge effects, particularly level-2 effects. can crop boundary region get final result free edge effects.
code: ee <- edge_effects(coarse, fact, method = "cubic") plot(ee, main = "Edge-effect zone (cubic method)")

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: lot differences three terraces methods boil assumptions shapes fine-scale surface estimated, none necessarily better others. can still put numbers question closely disaggregated results match true fine-scale raster particular DEM. Let’s mask edge band , method, compare estimate truth calculating maximum difference, average difference, difference overall elevation range. three terraces methods cluster closely overall accuracy, RMSE values around 14 m max errors near 70 m. Standard bilinear meaningfully worse — dramatically worse range, compressing elevation range nearly 50 m. terraces methods order differently range RMSE: disagg_bl() recovers true range closely (within 2 m), smoother disagg_cub() disagg_pyc() undershoot range ~25 m smoothness priors damp within-block peaks exist particular raster. Note fine-scale errors can’t go zero way coarse-scale errors shown , aggregation destroys information method can recover. fine-scale comparison measures close method gets unattainable lower bound; meaningful signal gap standard bilinear terraces methods, absolute magnitude individual number.
code: # Fine-scale comparison, masking edge zone for fair comparison rm <- mask(r, ee, maskvalues = 2)  as.data.frame(rm, xy = TRUE) |>   pivot_longer(-c(x, y, truth), names_to = "metric", values_to = "estimate") |>   filter(metric != "nearest") |>   mutate(metric = factor(metric, levels = method_levels, labels = method_labels)) |>   group_by(metric) |>   summarise(`max abs. error (m)` = max(abs(estimate - truth), na.rm = TRUE),             `RMSE (m)`   = sqrt(mean((estimate - truth)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),             `range error (m)` = diff(range(estimate)) - diff(range(truth)),             .groups = "drop") |>   arrange(`RMSE (m)`) |>   knitr::kable(digits = 3)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: defining property terraces methods re-aggregating fine output recovers original coarse values. Standard bilinear, contrast, . can verify aggregating fine output back coarse resolution comparing input. Let’s compare visually, also calculate accuracy statistics.

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: plot chunk residuals shows us several things: terra::disagg(method = "bilinear") substantial residuals spread across entire raster. Standard bilinear designed preserve block means. disagg_pyc() machine-precision residuals everywhere (essentially zero), confirming exact mass preservation construction. disagg_bl() small residual band confined boundary, 3×3 inverse kernel’s finite-radius approximation degrades. Interior residuals negligible. disagg_cub() also negligible error interior, shows slightly wider boundary band, consistent cubic’s larger 5×5 inverse kernel cubic interpolator’s longer boundary reach. consistent edge-effect band mapped previous section.
code: a <- aggregate(r, fact, fun = "mean", na.rm = TRUE)  to_df <- function(a) {       as.data.frame(a, xy = TRUE) |>             select(-nearest) |>             pivot_longer(-c(x, y, truth), names_to = "metric", values_to = "estimate") |>             mutate(metric = factor(metric, levels = method_levels, labels = method_labels)) } da <- to_df(a)  ggplot(da, aes(x, y, fill = estimate - truth)) +       geom_raster() +       facet_wrap(~ metric, ncol = 2) +       scale_fill_gradient2(mid = "gray95", name = "Error (estimate minus coarse input, m) ") +       coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE) +       guides(fill = guide_colorbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = .5)) +       theme(axis.title = element_blank(),             axis.text  = element_blank(),             axis.ticks = element_blank(),             legend.position = "top")

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: Let’s mask results boundary band calculate accuracy methods: reaggregation, disagg_pyc() essentially perfect mass preservation (machine precision). pre-sharpening methods also reconstruct coarse means extremely well, average error ~1 cm across DEM whose elevation range spans 800 m. residual error arises methods use finite-sized pre-sharpening filters truncate infinitely-large theoretically-perfect versions, analyses negligible compared sources uncertainty. standard bilinear interpolation nearly 30 m locations, 10 m average. errors non-random – biased toward compressing variability, case -representing coarse-scale elevation range ~24 m.
code: # Mask aggregated rasters with coarse-resolution mask coarse_mask <- aggregate(ee, fact, fun = "max", na.rm = TRUE) am <- mask(a, coarse_mask, maskvalues = 2)  to_df(am) |>       mutate(residual = abs(estimate - truth)) |>       group_by(metric) |>       summarise(`max abs. error (m)` = max(residual, na.rm = TRUE),                 `RMSE (m)` = sqrt(mean(residual^2, na.rm = TRUE)),                 `range error (m)` = diff(range(estimate)) - diff(range(truth)),                 .groups = "drop") |>       arrange(`max abs. error (m)`) |>       knitr::kable(digits = 3)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: coarse raster ’re working fairly small, code ran quite quickly. larger raster, compute speed can become issue affects choice method. Let’s quick benchmark comparing four methods speed. Instead using coarse raster input , ’s unrealistically small, ’ll disaggregate section “truth” raster. Let’s benchmark two versions disagg_pyc(): one fact = 32 one fact = 31. seemingly similar, trigger different behaviors function’s routine efficiently cascading intermediate resolutions. 32 best case scenario—’s power 2 optimal prime factorization, allowing function internally run six relatively fast steps fact = 2. 31 worst case scenario—’s prime can’t factorized sub-steps, function slog single big optimization requires lots iterations narrow smoothing kernel propagate information across high-resolution raster. plot chunk benchmark results specific particular scales example, rank order hold across situations, give reasonable sense relative speed functions. two pre-sharpening methods run order magnitude standard bilinear, add single cheap pre-filter pass coarse input raster; cubic slower bilinear mainly interpolation kernel bit wider. pycnophylactic methods substantially slower iterative convergence algorithms rather deterministic one-pass solutions. two pycno timings illustrate sensitivity algorithm exact value fact. ’re using pycno large raster large fact, flexibility fact, choosing composite values lots small prime factors can dramatically increase speed.
code: input <- crop(truth, ext(truth) * 0.75)  speed <- function(expression) as.vector(system.time(expression)["elapsed"])  times <- c(       standard_bilinear = speed(terra::disagg(input, 32, method = "bilinear")),       disagg_bl = speed(disagg_bl(input, 32)),       disagg_cub = speed(disagg_cub(input, 32)),       disagg_pyc_32 = speed(disagg_pyc(input, 32)),       disagg_pyc_31 = speed(disagg_pyc(input, 31)) )  times <- data.frame(method = names(times), seconds = times) |>       arrange(seconds) |>       mutate(method = factor(method, levels = method))  ggplot(times, aes(seconds, method, label = signif(seconds, 2))) +       geom_col(fill = "gray40") +       geom_text(hjust = 0, nudge_x = 2) +       theme_minimal() +       theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()) +       labs(x = "compute time (s)", y = NULL)

title: Comparing disaggregation methods on a digital elevation model
text: mass-preserving (“aggregation-consistent”) methods terraces substantially outperform standard bilinear interpolation disaggregation re-aggregation accuracy. expected given bilinear interpolation cell centers assumes cell values point estimates centroids rather block means cell area. three terraces methods differ mainly geometric assumptions. Bilinear pre-sharpening assumes fine-scale field best approximated piecewise linear planes connect coarse cell centers. Cubic pre-sharpening assumes best approximated locally smooth curves discontinuities kinks. Pycnophylactic disaggregation assumes ’s best approximated field minimizes isotropic global curvature. None assumptions strictly better others. mass-preserving methods, pycnophylactic interpolation (disagg_pyc()) highest round-trip accuracy, subject edge effects near raster boundaries, produces smoothest high-resolution surfaces consistent data. However, also slowest computationally, particularly fact readily decomposable prime factors. ’s good choice use case compute-constrained mass preservation near raster edges key. pre-sharpening methods (disagg_bl(), disagg_cub()) perform nearly well pycno typically run small fraction time. edge effects aware near raster boundaries, well tiny amount error across domain arises kernel truncation. cubic pre-sharpening method (disagg_cub()) produces results close pycno, small fraction compute time. many users, sensible default gives good accuracy good speed.

title: Introduction to terraces
text: ’ll use volcano dataset ships base R coarse input:  three methods takes coarse raster disaggregation factor:  three methods produce qualitatively similar output differ smoothness priors. See README method-comparison article guidance choosing .
code: coarse <- rast(volcano) |> aggregate(5) |> trim() plot(coarse, main = "Coarse input") fine_bl  <- disagg_bl( coarse, fact = 5) |> setNames("disagg_bl") fine_cub <- disagg_cub(coarse, fact = 5) |> setNames("disagg_cub") fine_pyc <- disagg_pyc(coarse, fact = 5) |> setNames("disagg_pyc")  plot(c(fine_bl, fine_cub, fine_pyc), nr = 1)

title: Introduction to terraces
text: pre-sharpening methods (disagg_bl() disagg_cub()) small boundary band mass preservation degrades slightly. can map affected zone edge_effects():  Values 1 2 indicate cells affected pre-sharpening filter boundary interpolation fallback, respectively. studies care precision near particular region interest, cleanest practical workaround disaggregate larger domain extends beyond study area, crop result — places edge effects safely outside cells actually use. Alternatively, need exact mass preservation everywhere — example, tiling mosaics extending domain isn’t possible — use disagg_pyc(), can slower large rasters isn’t subject effects.
code: ee <- edge_effects(coarse, fact = 5, method = "cubic") plot(ee, main = "Edge-effect zone (cubic method)")

title: Introduction to terraces
text: Re-aggregating fine output recover original coarse values. compare methods fairly, ’ll exclude boundary band pre-sharpening methods known degraded accuracy. build coarse-resolution mask flagging coarse cell contains level-2 boundary fine cell: disagg_pyc(), residuals machine precision — mass preserved exactly construction, without mask: disagg_bl() disagg_cub(), interior residuals small (~6 cm DEM covering 100 m range), nonzero due finite-radius kernel truncation: comparison, ’s check standard bilinear disaggregation using terra::disagg(). Residuals orders magnitude larger — standard bilinear isn’t mass-preserving:
code: coarse_mask <- aggregate(ee, 5, fun = "max")  max_error <- function(fine, coarse, mask){       back <- aggregate(fine, fact = 5, fun = "mean")       max(abs(values(mask(coarse - back, mask, maskvalues = 2))), na.rm = TRUE) } max_error(fine_pyc, coarse, coarse_mask) #> [1] 8.526513e-14 max_error(fine_bl, coarse, coarse_mask) #> [1] 0.06369263 fine_std <- terra::disagg(coarse, fact = 5, method = "bilinear") max_error(fine_std, coarse, coarse_mask) #> [1] 5.277122

title: Introduction to terraces
text: README explains package’s design helps choose methods. method-comparison article works detailed analysis real DEM, comparing methods based assumptions, accuracy, computational speed. Function help: ?disagg_bl, ?disagg_cub, ?disagg_pyc, ?edge_effects.

text: Kling M (2026). terraces: Conservation Empirical Statistics Raster Disaggregation. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/matthewkling/terraces.
code: @Manual{,   title = {terraces: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation},   author = {Matthew Kling},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/matthewkling/terraces}, }

what: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: terraces R package extends raster disaggregation methods terra provide Aggregation-Consistent Estimation algorithms. disaggregate coarse raster, typically two things want fine-scale result: consistent coarse raster (re-aggregating recovers input), consistent true fine-scale pattern underlying coarse raster (continuous, smooth local variation). Standard disaggregation methods sacrifice one goal . terraces methods satisfy . terraces provides three aggregation-consistent (mass-preserving) disaggregation methods whose output, re-aggregated, recovers original coarse values. include bilinear cubic variants pre-sharpening disaggregation (also known prefiltering) well implementation pycnophylactic interpolation (Tobler, 1979).

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("matthewkling/terraces")

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
code: library(terra) library(terraces)  coarse <- rast(nrows = 100, ncols = 100, vals = runif(10000))  # standard bilinear -- treats coarse values as point samples fine_std <- disagg(coarse, fact = 5, method = "bilinear")  # aggregation-consistent alternatives: fine_bl  <- disagg_bl(coarse, fact = 5) fine_cub <- disagg_cub(coarse, fact = 5) fine_pyc <- disagg_pyc(coarse, fact = 5)

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Standard interpolation methods (terra::disagg(method = "bilinear") similar functions packages) implicitly assume raster values point samples cell centers. terraces methods, contrast, treat averages cell’s area. Choose based data represent. Use terraces cell values represent averages area cell. typical case raster products: Coarsened DEMs (LiDAR aggregated coarser resolution) Climate model output reanalysis products (ERA5, CHELSA, WorldClim) Remote sensing products (radiance integrated pixel footprint) Gridded population counts density rasters raster produced terra::aggregate(fun = "mean") equivalent Use standard bilinear disaggregation less common case cell values represent point measurements cell centers, implied spatial extent. (Bilinear conventional choice case, though can’t recover extremes fall sample points.) Surfaces interpolated sparse point data (kriging, IDW, splines) Point-based model output grid cell centers Synthetic rasters generated evaluating continuous function grid points Coarsened rasters coarsening nearest-neighbor sampling

what: Which terraces method should I use?
title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: package includes three disaggregation methods, substantially accurate standard bilinear typical aggregated rasters. differ exactly preserve coarse-cell means. Pycnophylactic interpolation preserves exactly, construction. pre-sharpening methods almost exactly mass-preserving, contain tiny residual error resulting using finite-radius approximation ideal infinite inverse kernel. methods explained ,

previous_headings: Which terraces method should I use?
title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Use disagg_bl() want fast, drop-replacement terra::disagg(method = "bilinear") ’s aggregation-consistent. applies bilinear-style smoothness pre-sharpening filter coarse raster, non-iterative, runs similar speed terra::disagg(). works. Standard bilinear interpolation, treated linear operator “interpolate aggregate,” acts small smoothing convolution coarse grid. package derives smoothing kernel analytically (3×3 stencil), inverts direct linear solve, applies inverse single focal pass coarse raster standard terra::disagg(). inverse kernel depends disagg factor; ’s computed per factor cached session. structurally similar prefilter-based B-spline interpolation (Unser 1999), specialized case discrete data areal means rather point samples.

previous_headings: Which terraces method should I use?
title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Use disagg_cub() smoother output, bilinear’s piecewise structure visually objectionable want fast non-iterative method. applies Keys cubic convolution 5×5 pre-sharpening kernel. result smoother disagg_bl() (kinks cell centers), may overshoot near sharp gradients due cubic’s negative side lobes. works. Mathematically identical disagg_bl() 5×5 round-trip kernel (cubic interpolates 4×4 neighborhood, vs. 2×2 bilinear). package uses Keys cubic convolution standard parameter = -0.5, matching convention terra::resample(method = "cubic") (verified empirically). Note terra::disagg() expose cubic interpolation directly, disagg_cub() uses terra::resample() internally final interpolation step.

previous_headings: Which terraces method should I use?
title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Use disagg_pyc() want maximum smoothness. implements pycnophylactic interpolation (Tobler, 1979), adapted regular raster grids. result Laplacian-smooth (kinks anywhere) can stable near sharp gradients pre-sharpening methods. algorithm iterative typically substantially slower pre-sharpening methods large rasters. works. Standard Tobler (1979) iteration regular grid: start nearest-neighbor bilinear disaggregation, repeatedly smooth fine raster 3×3 Laplacian kernel, add per-block constant restore coarse block’s mean. Iterate convergence. default, iteration cascaded sequence smaller stages — example, disaggregating fact = 12 runs three sub-disaggregations factors 3, 2, 2. fact large, cascading much faster running single iteration full fact can give smoother results.

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: ’s 1D illustration standard linear interpolation versus three terraces methods. three terraces methods mass-preserving (aggregation-consistent), differ assumptions within-block structure.  depiction assumes start coarse input raster whose cell values (gray bars) represent averages likely unknown fine-scale pattern (blue curve). disaggreation methods estimates different fine-scale pattern (orange), theory compared truth assess accuracy. Re-aggregating fine-scale estimates coarse-scale means (red) fails recover input values standard interpolation, matches closely three mass-preserving methods.

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Pre-sharpened disaggregation boundary band mass preservation smoothness slightly degraded relative interior, resulting focal pass’s reflective extension terra’s boundary fallback cubic bilinear. typical raster sizes band minimal, can inspect edge_effects(), returns fine-resolution mask affected zone. Use drop affected cells results boundary reliability matters (e.g. small rasters, mosaic tiling, regions interest near edges). Pycnophylactic disaggregation milder version issue: smoother extends beyond raster boundaries, slightly reducing smoothness within ~1 coarse cell edge, mass preservation remains exact everywhere.

title: Conservation of Empirical Statistics in Raster Disaggregation
text: Tobler, W. R. (1979). Smooth pycnophylactic interpolation geographical regions. J. . Stat. Assoc. 74(367), 519–530. Keys, R. G. (1981). Cubic convolution interpolation digital image processing. IEEE Trans. Acoust. Speech Signal Process. 29(6), 1153–1160. Unser, M. (1999). Splines: perfect fit signal image processing. IEEE Signal Processing Magazine 16(6), 22–38.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
text: Disaggregates `coarse` finer resolution integer factor `fact`, aggregating result back block mean recovers input coarse values small truncation error governed `radius`. fine raster true bilinear surface — smoothness standard bilinear disaggregation, mass-preserving rather treating coarse values point samples cell centers.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
code: disagg_bl(   coarse,   fact,   radius = NULL,   max_radius_frac = 1/3,   na_fill = c("auto", "reflect", "fill") )

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
text: coarse SpatRaster. Multi-layer rasters supported (kernel applied layer turn). fact Integer disagg factor (>= 2). radius Integer half-width inverse kernel. `NULL` (default), `radius` set `max(7, fact + 2)`, targets relative round-trip error roughly 1e-5 1e-7 interior. Users rarely need change . Larger values reduce truncation error small one-time cost (kernel cached per `fact`); smaller values faster small rasters allow larger residual error. Automatically reduced small rasters; see `max_radius_frac`. max_radius_frac Numeric. Upper bound radius fraction coarse raster's smaller dimension. Defaults `1/3` keep boundary band (reflective extension approximate) dominating interior. Allowed range: (0, 0.5]. na_fill Boundary/NA handling mode passed [apply_kernel()]. `"auto"` (default) selects `"reflect"` rasters NAs `"fill"` rasters NAs.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
text: Fine SpatRaster. Layer names preserved.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
text: Internally: 1. Computes (looks cached) bilinear inverse kernel `fact`. 2. Applies kernel focal pass `coarse`, pre-sharpening    coarse raster subsequent standard bilinear disaggregation    produces result whose block means equal input. 3. Bilinear-disaggregates pre-sharpened coarse raster via    [terra::disagg()]. **Mass preservation approximate.** inverse kernel finite approximation ideal infinite operator, small truncation error remains. error concentrated cells within `radius` raster boundary; interior cells negligible error. Default radii target ~1e-5 ~1e-7 relative round-trip error interior. Setting larger `radius` reduces error small one-time cost (kernel cached per disaggregation factor). **Boundary handling.** focal pass uses reflective extension raster edges default. rasters NAs, `"fill"` mode pads nearest-valid values instead. See [edge_effects()] visualize fine cells fall edge-affected zone given inputs.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
text: Unser, M. (1999). Splines: perfect fit signal image processing. *IEEE Signal Processing Magazine* 16(6), 22-38.

title: Pre-sharpened bilinear disaggregation — disagg_bl
code: library(terra) #> terra 1.9.27 coarse <- rast(nrows = 30, ncols = 30, vals = runif(900))  # standard bilinear: not mass-preserving fine_std <- disagg(coarse, fact = 5, method = "bilinear") back_std <- aggregate(fine_std, fact = 5, fun = "mean") max(abs(values(coarse) - values(back_std)))   # substantial #> [1] 0.3261633  # pre-sharpened bilinear: mass-preserving up to truncation error fine_bl  <- disagg_bl(coarse, fact = 5) back_bl  <- aggregate(fine_bl, fact = 5, fun = "mean") max(abs(values(coarse) - values(back_bl)))    # small; reduced by larger radius #> [1] 0.1625402

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
text: Disaggregates `coarse` finer resolution integer factor `fact` using Keys cubic convolution interpolation, pre-sharpening filter makes result mass-preserving: aggregating back block mean recovers input coarse values small truncation error governed `radius`.

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
code: disagg_cub(   coarse,   fact,   radius = NULL,   max_radius_frac = 1/3,   na_fill = c("auto", "reflect", "fill") )

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
text: coarse SpatRaster. Multi-layer rasters supported. fact Integer disagg factor (>= 2). radius Integer half-width inverse kernel. `NULL` (default), `radius` set `max(9, fact + 4)`, slightly larger bilinear's default cubic's inverse kernel decays slowly. Users rarely need change . Larger values reduce truncation error small one-time cost (kernel cached per `fact`); smaller values faster small rasters allow larger residual error. Automatically reduced small rasters; see `max_radius_frac`. max_radius_frac Numeric. semantics [disagg_bl()]; upper bound kernel radius fraction coarse raster's smaller dimension. na_fill Character. Boundary/NA handling mode, options [disagg_bl()]: `"auto"` (default), `"reflect"`, `"fill"`.

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
text: Fine SpatRaster.

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
text: Compared [disagg_bl()]: Cubic produces smoother output (kinks cell centers,     bilinear ) modestly higher computational cost. Cubic negative side lobes may overshoot input range     near sharp gradients. data hard physical bounds     (precipitation, fractional cover), may matter. `radius`, cubic's truncation error somewhat     larger bilinear's inverse kernel decays     slowly. default `radius` correspondingly larger     compensate. **Mass preservation approximate.** Like [disagg_bl()], inverse kernel finite approximation ideal infinite operator. truncation error concentrated near raster boundary decays larger `radius`. Cubic's error equal radius somewhat larger bilinear's inverse kernel alternating-sign rings decay slowly bilinear's geometric decay. See [edge_effects()] visualize fine cells fall edge-affected zone given inputs. **Implementation note.** [terra::disagg()] expose cubic interpolation directly, `disagg_cub()` uses [terra::resample()] internally interpolation step, target fine grid nests perfectly within coarse cells. verified empirically `terra::resample(method = "cubic")` uses Keys cubic `= -0.5`, convention pre-sharpening kernel inverts.

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
text: Keys, R. G. (1981). Cubic convolution interpolation digital image processing. *IEEE Trans. Acoust. Speech Signal Process.* 29(6), 1153–1160.

title: Pre-sharpened cubic disaggregation — disagg_cub
code: library(terra) coarse <- rast(nrows = 30, ncols = 30, vals = runif(900)) fine_cub <- disagg_cub(coarse, fact = 5) back     <- aggregate(fine_cub, fact = 5, fun = "mean") max(abs(values(coarse) - values(back)))    # small; reduced by larger radius #> [1] 0.1026687

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: Iteratively smooths fine raster restoring coarse block's mean every iteration. result smooth surface whose block means equal input coarse values exactly — mass preservation enforced every iteration construction, approximated.

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
code: disagg_pyc(   coarse,   fact,   cascade = NULL,   max_iter = 100L,   tol = 1e-04,   smoother = c("laplacian_9", "laplacian_5"),   variant = c("additive", "multiplicative"),   init = c("near", "bilinear"),   na_fill = c("auto", "reflect", "fill"),   verbose = FALSE )

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: coarse SpatRaster. fact Integer disagg factor (>= 2). cascade Disaggregation stages. One : * `NULL` (default): auto-factorize `fact` prime factors,   sorted descending. example, `fact = 48` becomes   `c(3, 2, 2, 2, 2)`. stage's per-stage disaggregation   small, keeping iteration comfortable regime   3x3 smoother comparable bandwidth per-stage   block size. recommended default. * `fact` (single integer equal `fact`): single-stage   disaggregation. original Tobler (1979) formulation;   large `fact` converges slowly may show within-block   flattening (see "Caveat" ). * integer vector `prod(cascade) == fact`: explicit   factorization. Useful fine-tuning, e.g. `cascade = c(4, 4, 3)`   `fact = 48`. max_iter Integer, maximum iterations per stage. Default 100. tol Numeric, convergence tolerance fraction coarse raster's value range. Iteration stops largest per-cell change successive iterations falls `tol * range`. Default 1e-4. Controls close result smooth fixed point; mass preservation enforced exactly every iteration regardless convergence. smoother Character, smoothing kernel. One : * `"laplacian_9"` (default): full 3x3 mean, including diagonals. * `"laplacian_5"`: 5-point pattern (cardinal neighbors + center). variant Character, mean-restoration variant. One : * `"additive"` (default): add per-block constant. Works data. * `"multiplicative"`: scale within-block values. Strictly positive   data ; warn fall back additive block mean   non-positive. init Character, initial fine raster stage. One : * `"near"` (default): nearest-neighbor disagg. Matches original   Tobler formulation. * `"bilinear"`: standard bilinear disagg. smoother starting point   typically converges faster. na_fill Character, NA handling. semantics [disagg_bl()]; `"auto"` (default) uses `"fill"` coarse raster NAs, `"reflect"` otherwise. verbose Logical, print iteration progress. Default `FALSE`.

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: Fine SpatRaster, attributes `cascade` (stage sequence   actually used), `iterations` (integer vector, one per stage),   `converged` (logical vector, one per stage).

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: composite `fact` values, disaggregation cascaded sequence smaller stages default, substantially faster running full factor one shot produces smoother results. Compared pre-sharpening methods ([disagg_bl()], [disagg_cub()]): Pycnophylactic cubic produce smoother output bilinear     (kinks cell centers). Pycnophylactic *exactly* mass-preserving construction;     pre-sharpening methods approximately , small     truncation error concentrated near raster boundary.     small rasters boundary-sensitive applications, matters. Pycnophylactic tends produce less ringing near sharp     coarse-scale gradients [disagg_cub()]. Pycnophylactic iterative substantially slower     [disagg_bl()], though cascading typically much faster     one-shot pycno.

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: explicitly set `cascade = fact` (`fact` prime, leaving cascade options), algorithm runs single pycnophylactic iteration. large `fact` (> ~20 default 3x3 smoother), single-stage fixed point can show subtle within-block flattening: high-frequency content within coarse block becomes piecewise-constant block scale, smoother bridge across blocks single stage. structural property iterative algorithm regular grids, convergence issue — running iterations remove . default cascade behavior mostly eliminates issue keeping stage regime smoother's bandwidth matches per-stage block size. `fact` large prime factor (e.g. `fact = 22` = 2 x 11), 11-stage still exhibit artifact; consider [disagg_cub()] case.

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
text: Tobler, W. R. (1979). Smooth pycnophylactic interpolation geographical regions. *J. . Stat. Assoc.* 74(367), 519-530.

title: Pycnophylactic disaggregation (Tobler 1979) — disagg_pyc
code: library(terra) coarse <- rast(nrows = 30, ncols = 30, vals = runif(900)) fine_pyc <- disagg_pyc(coarse, fact = 6) # cascade = c(3, 2) back <- aggregate(fine_pyc, fact = 6, fun = "mean") max(abs(values(coarse) - values(back))) # ~ tolerance level #> [1] 4.440892e-16  # Single-stage -- original Tobler 1979 algorithm fine_tobler <- disagg_pyc(coarse, fact = 6, cascade = 6)

what: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
text: Returns fine-resolution mask showing cells affected boundary effects [disagg_bl()] / [disagg_cub()] output. Useful masking unreliable regions boundary-sensitive applications, inspecting affected zone processing.

title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
code: edge_effects(coarse, fact, method = c("bilinear", "cubic"), radius = NULL)

title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
text: coarse SpatRaster; coarse input passed [disagg_bl()] [disagg_cub()]. geometry used. fact Integer disagg factor (>= 2). method One `"bilinear"` `"cubic"`. Must match method intend use. radius Pre-sharpening kernel radius. `NULL`, default method used (matches [disagg_bl()] / [disagg_cub()] defaults). accurate masks, pass value pass disagg function.

title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
text: single-layer fine-resolution SpatRaster integer   values 0, 1, 2. geometry output   `disagg_*(coarse, fact)`. Attributes `method`, `radius`,   `interp_reach` attached inspection.

title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
text: Edge effects come two sources, encoded values 0, 1, 2: `0`: Interior cells edge effects. Mass preservation     interpolation accuracy full precision . `1`: Cells pre-sharpening focal pass used     reflective extension (directly via interpolation kernell     reaching reflected band). Mass preservation slightly     degraded interpolation full-order. `2`: Cells additionally affected terra's interpolation     boundary handling, cubic bilinear kernel     extend outside raster terra falls back lower-order     scheme. narrow band within ~0.5 coarse-cell-widths     edge bilinear, ~1.5 cubic. bands shrink nothing interior, large rasters bulk output `0`. small rasters, bands may overlap cover output; function still returns sensible mask case.

title: Visualize edge-effect regions in a disaggregated raster — edge_effects
code: library(terra) coarse <- rast(matrix(runif(30 * 30), 30, 30))  # See where edge effects would land for a fact=5 cubic disagg m <- edge_effects(coarse, fact = 5, method = "cubic") plot(m)   # Use the mask to drop edge-affected cells from a disagg result fine <- disagg_cub(coarse, fact = 5) fine_clean <- mask(fine, m, maskvalues = c(1, 2))

what: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel
title: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel
text: inverse kernel focal filter applied coarse raster standard interpolation; predistorts coarse data subsequent disagg produces fine raster whose block means equal input coarse values.

title: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel
code: kernel(method, fact, radius = NULL)

title: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel
text: method Character, prefilter method name. Currently `"bilinear"`. fact Integer disagg factor. radius Integer half-width kernel. Larger = accurate higher one-time cost. `NULL` uses method-specific default.

title: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel
text: square matrix side `2 * radius + 1`, attributes   `method`, `fact`, `radius`, `tail_max` (largest absolute value   kernel boundary; upper bound truncation error),   `conv_max_err` (largest deviation K * K_inv delta   interior; machine epsilon).

title: Compute the inverse kernel for a prefilter disaggregation method — kernel

what: Round-trip kernel for a disaggregation method — roundtrip_kernel
title: Round-trip kernel for a disaggregation method — roundtrip_kernel

title: Round-trip kernel for a disaggregation method — roundtrip_kernel
text: method Character. Currently `"bilinear"` (3x3 kernel) `"cubic"` (5x5 kernel, Keys convention = -0.5) supported prefilter methods. (Pycnophylactic disaggregation iterative round-trip kernel sense.) fact Integer disagg factor.

title: Round-trip kernel for a disaggregation method — roundtrip_kernel
